entry.title: When Business Asks for a Bicycle, Don't Build a Spaceship
entry.content:
When Business Asks for a Bicycle, Don't Build a Spaceship

I have developed a comprehensive playbook demonstrating how modern SAP tools and AI can deliver solutions in DAYS instead of MONTHS.

The shocking math:
- Traditional approach: 6 months, $650K in delayed value
- SAP tools + AI: 1 week, $25K in delayed value

This isn't just theory—it's a practical guide that covers:
- CDS Views for data modeling
- CAP for service generation
- Fiori Elements for instant UIs
- Integration Suite for connectivity
- AI to accelerate everything

https://www.linkedin.com/posts/rajesh-putumbaka-456267101_sap-btp-ai-development-playbook-ugcPost-7431029390173696001-y7Yn?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAABnYP8IBvlY4GDRmR8utVFLHXZ61gUvSa-0 
Download the full playbook (PDF attached) and share your thoughts: Have you encountered the "perfect is the enemy of good" trap in your SAP projects?
#SAP #DigitalTransformation #AI #SAPCommunity

entry.title: SAP User Experience Q1/2026 Update – Part 6: SAP Fieldglass
entry.content:
This sixth post in my series gives an overview of latest UX innovations in SAP Fieldglass, for managing your external workforce. The design of the supplier home page has been updated, and a number of further design updates have been made for a consistent SAP Business Suite experience. On-boarding is now made easy with WalkMe. Joule supports further use cases now, and also supports multiple languages, not just English. We have some nice embedded AI use cases, creating a better user experience, as well as many improvements in details that make a difference in day-to-day work.

In case you missed it, Part 1 provides general updates about Joule, SAP Work Zone and SAP Mobile Start.

I’ve grouped the UX innovations into these main sections:

  • Updated Supplier Home Page.
  • Design Updates for Suite Consistency.
  • Joule Innovotions.
  • Embedded AI for improved UX.
  • WalkMe for Easy On-Boarding.
  • Selected Usability Improvements in Applications
  • Further Information

 

Redesigned Supplier Home Page

The supplier home page is the first place that suppliers go to when using SAP Fieldglass, and hence it is crucial that it provides the most important information, actions and navigation options. Based on thorough user research, we have now redesigned the page to deliver a more streamlined and user-centered experience. At the same time, the new design also supports our focus on providing a consistent user experience across all products within the SAP Business Suite, as described in the next section.

Figure 1 shows an example. As you can see, the Announcement card is prominently visible. Users can choose to close it, in which case a button appears allowing them to re-open. The short video in Figure 2 shows what this looks like on a different example page.

You can configure the layout of the redesigned home page for each role with the Layout Manager, which was introduced in the May 2025 release. This learning journey tells you how to do it.

Figure 1: The redesigned supplier home page in SAP Fieldglass. Alt Text: Screen image showing a welcome banner at the top, and a collapsed vertical navigation bar running down the left side. The main screen has a section with pinned links, below which a selection of various cards are shown such as a list of My Work Items, Active Job Postings, Announcements. Two cards have an illustrated message that they have no data to display.

Figure 2: Hiding and re-opening the Announcement card. Alt Text: a short video showing the top half of the home page and the user clicking on “Close” and then “View Announcement”.

Find out more:

  • Smooth and Superior: The New, User-Centered Supplier Home Page.
  • Documentation: Redesigned Supplier Home Page.

 

Design Updates for Suite Consistency

We have made design improvements which are relevant for a consistent user experience across the entire SAP Business Suite, and which are now also available with SAP Fieldglass. As I mentioned in my April blog post Upcoming Design Updates for the SAP Business Suite (SAP Fiori for Web), consistency of the user experience across the various applications used by a user is an important part of overall usability, helping users to feel confident and at ease when using a suite of products – no unexpected surprises.

In this section, we’ll look at these updates (the first two cover the design updates for consistency):

  • Shell bar, navigation menu, user menu.
  • Redesigned user settings.
  • Design updates for more consistent applications.

Shell bar, navigation menu, user menu

Figure 1 above already features the new shell bar, with the collapsed side navigation bar on the left, showing a vertical list of icons representing menu entries. Users can expand the menu to better understand what each menu icon represents, as shown in Figure 3. This is done by clicking on the icon in the top left corner to the left of the SAP logo. It stays expanded until the user closes it, by clicking on the same icon again.

Figure 3 also shows the redesigned user menu, which follows the design for all SAP Business Suite products.

Figure 3: Navigation menu expanded, new user menu. Alt Text: screenshot of the home pages with the navigation menu on the left showing titles next to the icons, the user menu showing two entries “Settings” and “Legal”, and a separate “Sign Out” option at the bottom.

Redesigned User Settings

Selecting Settings in the user menu shown in Figure 3 above brings you to the redesigned settings page, shown in Figure 4 below. This design follows the SAP Fiori design guidelines, thus providing additional UX consistency across the suite. Beyond these visual aspects, it consolidates fields from the My Profile page and the My Preferences page into a single unified Settings page, which is easily accessed via the user menu. Some of the categories have been renamed too. Find out more.

  • Documentation: Updates to Settings Page.

Figure 4: Redesigned and consolidated user settings. Alt Text: Screenshot showing setttings, with full width cards “User Information” and “Appearance”, each with an “Edit” button top right.

Design Updates for More Consistent Applications.

SAP Fieldglass has also introduced the new illustrations from the SAP Design System (SAP Fiori). You can see some examples in Figures 1 and 2 above.

In addition to that, we have also aligned the design of individual applications with the design system. For example, the decision form list view now has the filters at the top of the page, and the Apply Filter button has been renamed to Go, thus aligning terminology – see Figure 5.

Figure 5: Decision form list view filters and Go button. Alt Text: Screen snippet showing “Decision Forms” above two rows of six and three filters. On the far right a highlighted “Go” button.

We have also moved the icons for table actions to the top of the decision form table, again for more consistency. Find out more in the documentation:

  • Decision Form List View UI Updates.

We have made similar updates in other applications, such as the project list view. You can find the full list in the What’s New documentaiton:

  • Project List View User Interface Updates
  • What’s New Viewer for SAP Fieldglass.

 

Joule innovotions

Now, you can use Joule:

  • in many languages, not just English;
  • to recommend job posting templates when create job postings;
  • to approve decision forms, including legal entity recognition.

Joule: Multi-Language Support

You can use Joule in all the languages available in SAP Fieldglass execept for Mongolian, which is currently not available in Joule. Figure 6 shows an example using French, which is used in many countries, not just France.

Figure 6: Joule multi-language support, showing an example in French. Alt Text: Three Joule images starting with a list of suggested prompts, the user chose “Créer une offre d’emploi”, then showing how Joule asks for details, the user entered “chef de projet”, Joule offered three templates, the user chose one and Joule shows the details. In the background a screenshot showing user preferences with the language French selected.

Not only can you interact with Joule in your native tongue, you can also use Joule to find job posting templates and statement of work templates that your company has translated into your language.

For companies that translate job postings and statement of work templates into multiple languages, Joule can automatically detect the user's preferred language and retrieve the corresponding localized template, if available. If not available in the user's preferred language, templates in the company-defined language will be returned. Find out more:

  • Documentation: Translated Template Retrieval in Joule.

Joule: Job Posting Template Recommendations

If you need to create a job posting, Joule can make this easier for you: after stating your requirements for the job, Joule will recommend the top three job posting templates that match best. Figure 7 shows an example where the user has asked for a “Project Manager with 3+ years experience”. In this example, Joule is in full-screen mode, giving space for details of a selected template to be shown on the right. The example shows that the user has clicked on the first suggestion in the list on the left.

Figure 7: Using Joule for SAP Fielglass to create a job posting for a contingent worker, with Joule providing top 3 matching job posting templates. Alt Text: Screenshot of Joule in full-screen mode, with prompts and outcome as described in the text.

Joule: Legal Entity Recognition

If Legal Entity functionality has been enabled in your SAP Fieldglass system, so that you can manage the external workforce for more than one company, you can now specify the legal entity as part of your requirement to Joule when creating job postings or statements of work. Figure 8 shows an example for creating a job posting for the legal entity “UK”.

Figure 8: Legal Entity Recognition by Joule for SAP Fieldglass when creating a job posting. Alt Text: Four Joule images, from left to right, with prompt “Create Job Posting”, Joule asks for details, then “programmer, legal entity is UK”, Joule shows 3 templates, user chose one, Joule asks to confirm, user says “yes”, Joule shows details for reviewing before creating.

Read more:

  • Documentation: Legal Entity Recognition in Joule.

Joule: Decision Form Approvals

When asking Joule for pending work items, Joule will now include Decision Form approvals too, as shown in Figure 9.

Figure 9: Joule for approving Decision Forms. Alt Text: Five Joule screenshots, user chooses “View my pending items”, Joule shows one “Contractor Vetting” pending approval, user selects “Approve Decision Form” from the context menu, Joule asks for a comment, user says “no comment”, decision form is approved.

More Information on Joule

Read this blog post and watch the video to learn more:

  • What’s New for Joule in SAP Fieldglass – November 2025.
  • Video: Introducing Joule in SAP Fieldglass (3:30 minutes).
  • Video: SAP Fieldglass 2025.11 Release Highlights Video - Joule (5:17 minutes).

 

Embedded AI for improved UX

AI-Assisted Skills-Based Hiring

When looking to hire consultants or contingent workers, you want to ensure that you get people with the right skills. Now, SAP Fieldglass makes this easy, with AI assistance.

AI helps you to specify which qualifications to ask for when creating a new job posting: AI-powered qualification recommendations uses AI to analyze your job title and description and then suggests relevant skills. The screen on the left in Figure 9 shows what this looks like.

AI helps you to consolidate qualifications that have different names for the same qualification. Suppliers describe the skills of their employees themselves, leading to a many different names for essentially the same skill. For example, “Azure Cloud,” “Azure”, and “MS Azure” could be used for someone with skills with Microsoft Azure Cloud. With the help of AI, these qualifications can be consolidated into one unique qualification. The screen top right of Figure 10 shows an example of how this is done.

Once skills have unique names, the other aspect that needs to be harmonized is the rating scale: some suppliers use 1 to 5, others perhaps 1 to 6 or 1 to 10. SAP Fieldglass normalizes these.

Having created a posting with appropriate skills, SAP Fieldglass helps you select candidates by making proposals based on the skills (and their ratings) that match, in addition to proposing candidates that have the appropriate job code. The image in Figure 10, bottom right, shows an example.

Figure 10: AI-powered qualification recommendations, qualification consolidation and skill-based candidate proposals in SAP Fieldglass. Alt Text: Three screen snippets. On the left a list of 6 recommended qualifications for skills such as Excel, Powerpoint, Word, with a relevancy shown as a percentage (55%, 50%, …). Top right a list showing Qualification “Microsoft Azure” as heading, with two entries below “Azure Cloud” and “MS Azure” and buttons “Consolidate Qualifications” and “Cancel”. Bottom right a list of 4 potential candidates, their names greyed out, with the qualification matching percentage shown in the far right column.

Read more in this blog post:

  • Smarter Skills-Based Hiring with SAP Fieldglass

Automated Approvals for Resource Time Sheets

Another example where AI can make users much happier is approving resource time sheets. In industries that have high volumes of worker time submissions, manually and dealing with discrepencies in the submitted time sheets can be tedious and time consuming. Now, you can define tolerance limits so that hours that fall within the defined tolerances are automatically approved. This reduces manual effort significantly, freeing up users to do more value-adding activities.

Read more about it:

  • Documentation: Automated Approvals for Resource Time sheets Leveraging Tolerance Limits.

 

WalkMe for Easy On-Boarding

First-time users can often feel a bit lost when using new business software, no matter how easy it is to use – simply the fact that it is unfamiliar can make people feel uncomfortable.

This is where WalkMe comes in, helping users discover how to use the product. SAP Fieldglass now provides WalkMe content out of the box. Figure 11 shows how users can choose from a list of topics. Figure 12 shows how it looks like if a user starts a topic (the welcome pop-over shown on the left), and how WalkMe gives users guidance for each step in the process (the image on the right).

Figure 11: Starting WalkMe in SAP Fieldglass. Alt Text: Screenshot of the home page with a full-height pop-over on the right with heading “Type in your question” above a text entry field, below that sections “General”, “Contingent Workforce”, “Services” with a list of entries such as “Review work items” or “Create a job posting”.

Figure 12: WalkMe examples in SAP Fieldglass. Alt Text: Two screenshots with pop-overs. On the left: “Review work items” with a description and a “Guide me” button; on the right “Approve Time Sheet” pop-over with a small popover connected to the “Approve” button, explaining what to do.

Find out more:

  • Documentation: WalkMe Walkthroughs Available in SAP Fieldglass.

 

Selected Usability Improvements in Applications

Applications in SAP Fieldglass are constantly improving, in many cases not just with new features and functions but also with aspects that improve the overall user experience. Even if they are only improvements in details: the details matter if you want to provide a good experience, since if you get them wrong, they can be very annoying.

Below are some highlights for these improvements in details.

Reusable Resource Types for faster resource creation
Rather than having to always manually enter all the data for a new contractor or piece of equipment, you can define those entries that you want to have pre-filled, by defining reusable resource types. For example you can define address, job code, time tracking, work schedule, activity checklist and more just once, and then use this to pre-fill your fields when creating a new resource. Find out more here:

  • Standardize Resource Creation with Reusable Resource Types in SAP Fieldglass

Tailored list views based on user roles
List views are core to daily operations for teams managing job postings, work orders, and statements of work. However, only some of the many columns shown are relevant to a user in specific role. Although users could customize their views, it turns out that this is rarely done. Now, customers can define views per role, specifying which columns to show and which to hide. This supports faster, more focused decisions and delivers a cleaner, more intuitive experience. Find out more:

  • Documentation: Customized List Views.

One-click line item copy for purchase orders
Here is a nice little detail that will make users smile: with one click, you can now copy the previous line item when creating a purchase order. In many procurement scenarios the same services are purchased regularly, so it is very helpful for users to copy an item and then make only the few necessary changes before continuing with the next item. As you can see in Figure 13, we have introduced a “copy” icon to duplicate the existing PO line.

Figure 13: One-click duplication of purchase order line. Alt Text: a screen snippet showing a PO line with the icon for copying the line on the left of the line.

Making it easier for suppliers to manage line items
We have introduced a number of improvements for suppliers working on SoWs (Statements of Work). For example, when purchasing contracts are in place, it makes sense to include information from the contract in a purchase order to ensure compliance, accurate pricing and so on. Navigating to the right information in the contract when creating a purchase order has been a challenge – but no longer! Suppliers can search and insert contract segments in any order now. Find out more:

  • Documentation: Supplier Workflow Enhancements on SOW Characteristics Tab.

Making it easier for suppliers to maintain charges against PO items
In asset-intensive and plant maintenance industries, suppliers frequently need to submit or resubmit charges against PO lines. This is especially common when working with complex SOWs that involve multiple tasks and services. Up until now, suppliers encountered multiple navigation paths when adding or correcting line items, particularly after a charge is rejected due to issues like incorrect pricing or missing details. Now, this has been made significantly easier: all relevant actions are accessible no matter where they begin the process. Figure 14 shows an example.

Figure 14: Supplier access to all actions when managing PO line charges. Alt Text: two screenshots, showing the card “Corrective maintenance repairs – Engineering Repairs Services and Parts” in different contexts, on the left with an “Edit” button, on the right with an “Itemize” button.

 

Further Information

For an overview of the major new features with the 2511 release, have a look at:

  • SAP Fieldglass 2025.11 Release Spotlight Features Guide.

These videos are available too:

  • SAP Fieldglass 2025.11 Release Highlights Video - Joule
  • SAP Fieldglass 2025.11 Release Highlights Video - Configuration Manager
  • SAP Fieldglass 2025.11 Release Highlights Video - General Updates
  • SAP Fieldglass 2025.11 Release Highlights Video - Services Procurement

Have a look at all the latest updates, month by month, in the

  • What’s New Viewer for SAP Fieldglass.
  • This blog post explains how to use the What’s New Viewer:
    SAP Fieldglass November 2025 Release Communication Now Available!

Another good place to stay up to date is the SAP Fieldglass page on SAP Community.

 

Continue Reading

I hope you enjoyed this sixth part, covering SAP Fieldglass. Here's a link to part seven next, covering latest UX innovations for SAP Analytics Cloud:

  • SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 7: SAP Analytics Cloud.

Part 1 of the series lists all the parts, and links to those parts that have already been published:

  • SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start.

Do keep posting your experiences and recommendations yourself in the SAP Community, with the SAP Fiori and/or the User Experience tag! In case you are wondering how to get a list of the most recent blog posts on SAP Fiori and User Experience in our new SAP Community, use these links:

  • Latest blog posts on SAP Fiori in the SAP Community.
  • Latest blog posts on User Experience in the SAP Community.

For general information on design at SAP and SAP Fiori, check out:

  • www.sap.com/design.
  • www.sap.com/fiori    
  • SAP Community: SAP Fiori topic page.

entry.title: Gregor Wolf
entry.content:
About Gregor

  • Germany | SAP Mentor since 2007 | Follow Gregor on LinkedIn

I am an independent SAP solution architect, developer and SAP Mentor. I focus on SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP), Node.JS, SAP Fiori Elements and Security. I've supported several customers during their SAP Cloud ERP Private Edition (S/4HANA On Premise) implementation. Since the end of 2024 I support the technical topics at an SAP Cloud ERP Public Edition Customer.

Topics of interest: SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP), SAP Cloud ERP public and private, SAPUI5, OpenUI5, SAP Fiori, SAP HANA Cloud

Gregor, what inspired you to become an SAP Mentor?

A key influence early in my SAP Community journey was Mark Finnern. He is the father of the SAP Community. Mark encouraged me to move beyond answering questions and start sharing detailed technical how-to's but also event reports in blog posts. In 2007 Mark also founded the SAP Mentors program which gave me the opportunity to provide structured, experience-based feedback to SAP product teams and help bridge the gap between product vision and real-world implementation.

What advice would like to share with other SAP community members?

My advice to other SAP Community members is simple: contribute consistently and think long-term.

Start by answering questions and helping others solve real problems - but don’t stop there. Document your lessons learned, share architectural insights, and write about what worked and what didn’t. That’s how collective expertise grows. And you will be amazed how often you research years later and find your own answers and blog posts to solve a problem you're just facing. Despite all the new stuff SAP has quite a long tail that doesn't change quickly.

Engage constructively, challenge respectfully, and focus on signal over noise. The community is not just a support forum - it’s an ecosystem where practitioners can influence product direction, elevate standards, and shape the future of SAP together.

entry.title: Configure Your SAP Fiori Elements Apps: Introducing Fine-Tuning in the SAP Fiori Development Portal
entry.content:
Throughout our blog series on the SAP Fiori development portal, we've explored how SAP Fiori elements leverages metadata and annotations to build enterprise-grade UIs following the Global UX design principles. But what happens when you need to adjust UI behavior in ways that aren't expressed through annotations? That's what we call configuration – and the Fiori tools are the best place for it. But we're excited to introduce a new feature in the SAP Fiori development portal that makes configuration discovery and experimentation easier than ever.

Understanding Fine-Tuning in SAP Fiori Elements

SAP Fiori elements applications are primarily driven by metadata and annotations - this is their superpower. Annotations define what data to display, how to structure it, and which features to enable. However, not every UI behavior can or should be controlled through annotations. Some aspects require configuration settings that fine-tune how building blocks render and behave.

Let's take the table building block as a perfect example. When you annotate your service with UI.LineItem, SAP Fiori elements automatically selects an appropriate table type based on your entity's capabilities and the Global UX design guidelines:

  • If your entity set is analytics-enabled, an Analytical Table is displayed, providing built-in aggregation and grouping capabilities
  • For standard use cases, the Responsive Table is shown by default - it's optimized for various screen sizes and delivers the best user experience across devices
  • But what if you need to display massive datasets where scrolling performance is critical? The Grid Table excels at rendering huge amounts of data efficiently

Here's the challenge: there's no annotation to switch between these table types. This is where fine-tuning comes into play. You configure these settings in your application's manifest.json or directly in the building block definition when using XML views. But how do you discover what's configurable? What are the available options? What values can you set?

Introducing the Fine-Tuning Panel in the SAP Fiori Development Portal

The SAP Fiori development portal now includes a dedicated Fine-Tuning panel that answers these questions interactively. When you open the code editor for any building block - let's use the table as our example - you'll notice two tabs at the top: "Code" and "Fine-Tuning."

Fine-Tuning Panel

The Fine-Tuning panel reveals all available configuration settings for the building block, complete with:

  • Clear descriptions explaining what each setting controls
  • The current value applied in the sample
  • A searchable interface to quickly find specific settings
  • Immediate visual feedback when you change values

Exploring Fine-Tuning: A Table Story

Let's walk through a practical scenario. Navigate to the Table Building Block in the SAP Fiori development portal and click "Show Code" to open the code editor. Now switch to the "Fine-Tuning" tab.

You'll see a comprehensive list of configuration options. Let's say you're working on an application that needs to display thousands of travel records, and scrolling performance is becoming an issue. You remember hearing about the Grid Table's superior performance with large datasets, but you're not sure how to enable it.

In the Fine-Tuning panel, use the search box and type "type." The list immediately filters to show the "type" setting, which controls the table visualization. You'll see:

  • A description explaining the table type setting
  • The current value: "ResponsiveTable" (the default)
  • A dropdown showing all available options: ResponsiveTable, GridTable and AnalyticalTable

Searching for settings

Change the value to "GridTable" and watch the magic happen. The preview immediately updates, showing your table rendered as a Grid Table.

Changing the table type

You can see the visual differences

  • the denser layout
  • the different scrolling behavior
  • the optimized rendering for large datasets

But here's where it gets even better. Switch back to the "Code" tab, and you'll notice the code has been automatically updated:

<core:FragmentDefinition
xmlns:core="sap.ui.core"
xmlns="sap.m"
xmlns:macros="sap.fe.macros">
   <macros:Table metaPath="/Travel/@com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.LineItem" type="GridTable"/>
</core:FragmentDefinition>

The `type="GridTable"` attribute has been added automatically. If you're using building blocks directly in your XML views, you can simply copy this code into your application. Perfect for custom page development!

From Exploration to Implementation

The Fine-Tuning panel serves as your bridge from exploration to implementation. You can experiment with different settings, understand their effects, and see the exact code needed - all in one interactive environment.

However, it's important to understand where these settings belong in your application architecture:

  • For Building Blocks in XML Views (like in custom pages): Apply the settings directly as attributes on the building block, just as shown in the code editor. The portal gives you the exact syntax you need.
  • For Standard Floorplans (List Report, Object Page, etc.): The table building blocks are already integrated into the page structure. To fine-tune them, you add configuration settings to your application's manifest.json file. The SAP Fiori tools Page Map provides a user-friendly interface for configuring these settings without manually editing JSON.

This two-path approach ensures you can fine-tune your application whether you're building custom pages with building blocks or extending standard floorplans.

The Fiori Development Portal as Your Fine-Tuning Laboratory

Think of the SAP Fiori development portal's Fine-Tuning panel as your laboratory for configuration experimentation. You can:

  • Discover settings you didn't know existed
  • Understand the purpose and impact of each configuration option
  • Test different combinations to find the optimal setup for your requirements
  • Learn the exact syntax needed for implementation
  • Make informed decisions backed by visual evidence

This interactive approach transforms configuration from a trial-and-error process into a guided, informed decision-making experience.

But keep in mind: SAP Fiori elements applies Global UX design principles automatically. Only fine-tune when you have specific requirements that justify deviating from the defaults.

What's Next

The Fine-Tuning panel represents another step in our commitment to making SAP Fiori elements development more accessible, efficient, and collaborative. But we're not stopping here. We have exciting enhancements planned that will further improve your development experience with the SAP Fiori development portal.

Stay tuned for upcoming blog posts where we'll introduce additional features that continue to bridge the gap between exploration and implementation, making your journey from concept to production even smoother.

To get the full overview of the SAP Fiori development portal, check out the following blog posts of the mini-series:

  • Introducing the SAP Fiori Development Portal: Your Gateway to Rapid SAP Fiori App Creation (1 of 6)
  • Get familiar with SAP Fiori development portal and the power of the building blocks (2 of 6)
  • Explore the potential of SAP Fiori development portal using complex building blocks (3 of 6)
  • Use SAP Fiori development portal to understand extension options for standard floorplans (4 of 6)
  • Prepare building custom pages by using SAP Fiori development portal (5 of 6)
  • Bringing the SAP Fiori development portal and its use to the next level (6 of 6)

entry.title: Future SAP apps will be transient - prove me wrong!
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • What is a transient app
  • Prompting
  • Benefit to user
  • Objections and Concerns
  • Cost of code
  • Training
  • Consistency across users and time
  • Scenario
  • Traditional apps (today)
  • Transient apps (future)
  • Summary

Introduction

Predicting the future is a fool’s errand! Things change at a fast pace, so we generally do not try and predict the winner of a sporting tournament 10 years in advance – unless we’re trying to stoke discussion. That’s my goal with this post!

 

What is a transient app

A transient app is an app that is written on-the-fly and lasts only during the session in which it is used. It is then removed from all memory as if it never existed. The concept of a transient app arose in the AI community around 2024/25 and is well described by a comparison to traditional apps as follows:

 

If a traditional app is like a restaurant you must travel to, a transient app is like a personal chef who appears in your kitchen, cooks exactly what you want, and cleans up so perfectly you wouldn't know they were there.

 Important:  Transient apps exist in a new world where software creation approximates being both free and instant!

In the general world of AI, a transient app can pull data from various sources and so is not restricted to a single system like SAP.  Anthropic’s Model Context Protocol (MCP) is currently the best example of how this is done. We will, however, assume an entirely SAP ecosphere. Hence, we are considering what could be called SAP transient apps – Lets call them STAs for short.

Prompting

Prompting is an important concept in the world of AI including our future STA scenario. There must always be an interface with this SAP AI we’re imagining where a user can give “prompts”. This may be text-based (probably how you currently use your favourite LLM) or a verbal prompt or something else ( Neuralink?  😯 ) .

 

Benefit to user

For now, let’s ignore all objections to whether an STA is possible and look only at why a user would even want such a thing. Why not just have a standard interface that everyone uses i.e. what we have today?

Let’s look at some reasons using the simple example of a timesheet STA:

  1. The AI will learn to know what a user’s taste is in terms of UI. This is tweaked as user’s respond with prompts informing how a presented UI can be improved . One user may prefer a table like interface . Another might prefer tabs for each day with various colours, public holidays flashing, birthday reminders etc. Yet another might prefer a more wizard-like approach. Remember this is a simple example. A more complicated app will have more possible UI variations.

      2.  A UI may not be needed at all!  A user may be happy to give the prompt “log that I worked on all workdays this week”.  A UI-less STA can be generated that does this task. I'd use this option!

 

Objections and Concerns

This is the big one! Why STAs might seem like a pipedream – and why I think they are not.

 

Cost of code

The most fundamental hurdle may be our understanding of the cost of code. In our current world code requires human expertise and therefore has dollar costs and time costs. It is too valuable to throw out once its created.

 

Think of the darning kits that some of us can remember being around many years ago. They contained what was needed to repair a hole in a sock. Once socks became cheaper it became easier to “just buy a new pair” than sit down and spend time repairing. Just as socks have lost their value ( but not importance ) to us,  AI will make code less valuable ( but still important ) because it’s both quick and cheap to create.

 

This concept of code being created instantly is reminiscent of SAP’s move to HANA that imagined a world where there were no time costs to access data. Turns out that we still need to care about optimisation in DB selects because the time costs are not absolutely zero. However, the speed is sufficiently fast that our S4 ecosphere works well with the reduced attention on data select loads. No doubt there will be such nuances ahead where we find code to be not entirely “free and instant” but close enough to make a difference.

 

Training

How do you train people to use something when you don’t know exactly how the UI they will use looks? I’ll make the answer to this one short. The user can be walked through any given UI presented to them by the AI itself (like a chatbot). On the first few uses of a UI to do a particular task they will probably request changes to the UI to suite their preferences. This will be remembered by the AI so that the user is not always seeing a different UI for a task that is done repeatedly. Even a brand-new task can result in a UI based on preferences requested by the user on previous tasks e.g. a user might prefer that wizard style flow in the UI. The AI gets to know you!

 

Consistency across users and time        

This is the question of whether one transient app is doing exactly the same thing as another transient app. It is a question more likely to be asked by an SAP expert than a user.

How do we know:

  1. That any 2 users are subject to the exact same business rules if they are using different STAs?
  2. Why certain data was changed in a certain way at a certain point in time? How  can we reproduce that to either audit or debug an issue if the STA that created the data is gone?

These concerns arise because we think in terms of traditional apps (whether Fiori apps, Web Dynpro apps or WEBGUI transactions).

Our traditional apps have business rules embedded in them! Even though we claim to abstract them away using BRF, public methods or something else the reality is that they are embedded in our code throughout. Let’s look at a simple example to contrast the current traditional world with the transient world of the future.

 

Scenario

Materials of type “Dangerous Goods” will be added to the system. Only users with the role DGA (Dangerous Goods Approved) will be able to work with these materials. Anywhere, such a material is added to a document (like when creating a Purchase Order) the user must for legal reasons check a checkbox saying they have considered storage implications.

 

Traditional apps (today)

The Functional consultant will put the scenario above in a spec. They will then track down all UIs that allow a user  to create a document that uses materials. They may find (for our example) 3 such apps (the PO create Fiori app, a PO create transaction and a Stock Transfer Order app to move materials between warehouses). They will then instruct security to create the role and assign to a list of people. They will ask the technical team to restrict materials available in the 3 apps to non-dangerous goods for unauthorised users – this can be done on the backend for all 3 apps (exits in DPC classes, BADIs, implicit enhancements etc).

On the frontend, developers will be asked to add the checkbox whenever a dangerous good is selected by authorised users and to confirm it has been checked before allowing save/submit of the document.

Note that this new requirement is embedded into both backend and frontend code! If a new custom app is created that works with materials, the functional consultant will need to remember this requirement to add it in. If it is not the same functional consultant then hopefully they’ve read a document somewhere that told them of this requirement (or it was in a knowledge transfer they were given 😆😆).

Transient apps (future)

A functional consultant will declare the new requirement via an AI prompt. The prompt will basically be the scenario paragraph above. When the AI then asks exactly which users should have the new authorisation for dangerous goods the response could be something like  “everyone in the current role ABC plus those in role DEF but don’t make a composite of these roles as this might change. Just create a new DGA role containing the current union of ABC and DEF”.

The rules will be extracted completely into a rules engine. This rules engine does not need to be human readable (like a BRF). That said, any authorised person can query this rules engine, and the results will be presented in a transient SAP app which will not only be human readable but optimised for that particular user's taste.

Any user creating a PO, say, will be subject to the rules and they will be enforced by the transient PO Creation app they use in their session.

We see a secret to the transient app – the business rules are completely abstracted. We are left with only 3 persisting objects:

  1. Data (transaction, master & config)
  2. Business engine (containing business rules)
  3. AI engine

The apps themselves don’t “live” anywhere. Some data and all business rules are timestamped. That way we could reproduce a transient app the way it would have run 2 years ago. Sure, the colours and other preferences may look different to what it did to the user that did something 2 years ago but the resulting behaviour is identical. 

Summary

Future SAP users will not be content with a prescribed app layout combined with a handful of themes like Quartz Light or Horizon Dark. They will insist on transient apps creating UIs that precisely match their personal preferences!

 

OK let's see how that ages...

entry.title: Customize SAPUI5 apps for business users – make an impact by joining our CEI project
entry.content:
SAP Customer Engagement Initiatives (CEI) on https://influence.sap.com allow you, as an SAP customer or partner, to gain early insights into SAP's product developments and work directly with SAP teams to shape future product directions. This project presents a unique opportunity to work with SAPUI5 flexibility product managers and engineering teams, leveraging your expertise customizing SAPUI5 apps with key user adaptation.

Join the upcoming CEI project to make an impact on how key users interact with key user adaptation

If you’re already convinced – join here. If not, read on 🙂

What is key user adaptation?

Key user adaptation is a mode that allows extending SAPUI5 applications for other users right in the browser. It is highly intuitive and requires no technical or programming skills. Just use drag and drop to make UI changes – no modification or copy of SAP standard coding necessary! Read more here.

What is the targeted improvement?

Although the product is mature and widely used, we actively evolve it by delivering new functionality and capability enhancements alongside continuous user experience improvements, informed by UI testing and direct customer feedback. 

We are in the process of overhauling key user adaptation’s UI, simplifying the overall approach while making often-used scenarios more streamlined. We’ve taken a close look at the version history (of activated adaptations) and how our users interact with it, extended the “embed third-party content” feature, widened the scope of custom fields that can be added to the UI – and more.

To ensure that we are on the right way, we need your help!

Who should participate?

Users who want to adapt standard SAPUI5 apps for business users. Both key users and technical users are welcome.

Why should you participate?

  • Gain understanding of SAPUI5 flexibility
  • Chance to influence a central SAPUI5 feature
  • Early access to prototypes

How can you participate?

Don’t miss out on this opportunity to share your feedback and engage with SAPUI5 flexibility development teams. Register by March 15, 2026, and make a true impact on how you can customize SAP apps to your needs!

Click here to sign up for our project. (A short overview about how to get started on influence.sap.com can be found here).

After you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call where you will get more information. This call is followed by individual or grouped feedback sessions.

Thank you in advance for helping us shape the future of SAPUI5 flexibility!

entry.title: Enhancing Standard CDS Views in FIORI My Inbox
entry.content:
Introduction:

SAP Fiori My Inbox is a powerful application designed for efficient task and workflow management within the SAP ecosystem. The information about the specific work item was not sufficient for the approvers to proceed further, hence added additional details to the standard screen. This enhancement allows structured business data, often residing in the SAP HANA database, to seamlessly become part of the Fiori user experience. In this blog, we will explore how to enhance Fiori My Inbox by extending a standard Core Data Services (CDS) view.

Objective:

The primary goal is to enhance the standard Fiori My Inbox by extending the standard CDS view, ensuring a unified interface for managing and processing workflows. The journey involves several key steps, and we will walk through each of them to achieve a streamlined workflow experience.

Steps to Follow:

Step 1: Create an extended view and add Virtual element.

Begin by creating and activating a CDS view with the required annotations. Include virtual elements to access any ABAP logic inside CDS view. 

For this we have extended the standard CDS view C_PurchaseOrderFs and implemented additional fields using virtual elements concept.

Sample Code:

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewAppendName: 'ZC_PURCHASEORDER'
@EndUserText.label: 'Extension on Standard C_PurchaseOrderFs'

extend view C_PurchaseOrderFs with ZFI_C_PURCHASEORDERFS_EXT

association [1..1] to ZFI_MY_INBOX_REQUEST_DETL_CDS as _Requester on I_PurchaseOrderEnhanced.purchaseorder = _Requester.PoNo

{
  @ui.fieldGroup: [{
    qualifier: 'Recipient',
    groupLabel: 'Recipient', groupLabel_asOtr: 'FA163EDF73161EE587C742C45B2B0F3D',
    position: 40,
    exclude: false,
    importance: #HIGH }, {
        qualifier: 'Detail3',
        groupLabel: 'Recipient', groupLabel_asOtr: 'FA163EDF73161EE587C742C45B2B0F3D',
        position: 40,
        exclude: false,
        importance: #HIGH
    }]
  @EndUserText.label: 'Requested By'
  _Requester.RequestedBy         as RequestedBy,

  @ui.fieldGroup: [{
    qualifier: 'Recipient',
    groupLabel: 'Recipient', groupLabel_asOtr: 'FA163EDF73161EE587C742C45B2B0F3D',
    position: 50,
    exclude: false,
    importance: #HIGH }, {
        qualifier: 'Detail3',
        groupLabel: 'Recipient', groupLabel_asOtr: 'FA163EDF73161EE587C742C45B2B0F3D',
        position: 50,
        exclude: false,
        importance: #HIGH
    }]
  @EndUserText.label: 'Requested Department'
  _Requester.RequestedDepartment as RequestedDepartment,

  @ui.fieldGroup: [{
  qualifier: 'Recipient',
  groupLabel: 'Recipient', groupLabel_asOtr: 'FA163EDF73161EE587C742C45B2B0F3D',
  position: 60,
  exclude: false,
  importance: #HIGH }, {
      qualifier: 'Detail3',
      groupLabel: 'Recipient', groupLabel_asOtr: 'FA163EDF73161EE587C742C45B2B0F3D',
      position: 60,
      exclude: false,
      importance: #HIGH
  }]
  @EndUserText.label: 'Buyer Name'
  _Requester.BuyerName           as BuyerName,

  @EndUserText.label: 'Buyer Note'
  @ObjectModel.readOnly: true
  @ObjectModel.virtualElement: true
  @ObjectModel.virtualElementCalculatedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_MM_PO_BUYER_NOTE'

  @ui.fieldGroup: [{
  qualifier: 'Recipient',
  groupLabel: 'Recipient', groupLabel_asOtr: 'FA163EDF73161EE587C742C45B2B0F3D',
  position: 70,
  exclude: false,
  importance: #HIGH }, {
      qualifier: 'Detail3',
      groupLabel: 'Recipient', groupLabel_asOtr: 'FA163EDF73161EE587C742C45B2B0F3D',
      position: 70,
      exclude: false,
      importance: #HIGH
  }]
  cast('' as abap.char(1333))    as BuyerNote,

  @EndUserText.label: 'Supplier Participants'
  @ObjectModel.readOnly: true
  @ObjectModel.virtualElement: true
  @ObjectModel.virtualElementCalculatedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_MM_PO_SUPPL_NOTE'

  @ui.fieldGroup: [{
  qualifier: 'Recipient',
  groupLabel: 'Recipient', groupLabel_asOtr: 'FA163EDF73161EE587C742C45B2B0F3D',
  position: 80,
  exclude: false,
  importance: #HIGH }, {
      qualifier: 'Detail3',
      groupLabel: 'Recipient', groupLabel_asOtr: 'FA163EDF73161EE587C742C45B2B0F3D',
      position: 80,
      exclude: false,
      importance: #HIGH
  }]
  cast('' as abap.char(1333))    as SupplierParticipants,

  @EndUserText.label: 'Supplier Note'
  @ObjectModel.readOnly: true
  @ObjectModel.virtualElement: true
  @ObjectModel.virtualElementCalculatedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_MM_PO_SCOPE_NOTE'


  @ui.fieldGroup: [{
  qualifier: 'Recipient',
  groupLabel: 'Recipient', groupLabel_asOtr: 'FA163EDF73161EE587C742C45B2B0F3D',
  position: 90,
  exclude: false,
  importance: #HIGH }, {
      qualifier: 'Detail3',
      groupLabel: 'Recipient', groupLabel_asOtr: 'FA163EDF73161EE587C742C45B2B0F3D',
      position: 90,
      exclude: false,
      importance: #HIGH
  }]
  cast('' as abap.char(1333))    as ScopeWork,

  @EndUserText.label: 'Approver'
  @ObjectModel.readOnly: true
  @ObjectModel.virtualElement: true
  @ObjectModel.virtualElementCalculatedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_MM_PO_APPROVERS_LIST'

  @ui.fieldGroup: [{
  qualifier: 'Recipient',
  groupLabel: 'Recipient', groupLabel_asOtr: 'FA163EDF73161EE587C742C45B2B0F3D',
  position: 100,
  exclude: false,
  importance: #HIGH }, {
      qualifier: 'Detail3',
      groupLabel: 'Recipient', groupLabel_asOtr: 'FA163EDF73161EE587C742C45B2B0F3D',
      position: 100,
      exclude: false,
      importance: #HIGH
  }]
  cast('' as abap.char(1333))    as ApproverID1
}

We need to mention the SADL class using an annotation @ObjectModel.virtualElementCalculatedBy. 

Activate the CDS View to ensure that no discrepancies to the existing code.

Step 2: Create enhancement class in ABAP.

Create mentioned enhancement class with the interface ‘IF_SADL_EXIT_CALC_ELEMENT_READ’.

Implement the method ‘IF_SADL_EXIT_CALC_ELEMENT_READ~CALCULATE’

Define annotations using the Annotation Modeler to provide additional information about the service metadata. 

Step 3: Load Metadata

Ensure the new fields are added to the metadata, load the metadata using the transaction /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE.

 Output:

The above is final output of how we modified this Fiori My Inbox, we can visualize the previous configuration and results after the enhancement.

By providing a unified and user-friendly interface like this, we can enhance efficiency and user satisfaction in handling tasks and approvals.

Before enhancement:

After enhancement:

Summary:

In conclusion, enhancing the standard CDS view in Fiori My Inbox transforms task and workflow management. The seamless interaction with business data in real-time contributes to an overall streamlined workflow experience within the organization.

By following these steps, organizations can unlock the full potential of Fiori My Inbox, optimizing their workflow processes and improving collaboration across various backend systems and applications.

entry.title: Reference Tiles in SAP Fiori: The Smart Way to Share Apps
entry.content:
Introduction: Ever found? You've got a perfectly good Fiori app that works great for one team, but now another team wants the exact same app in their workspace, The answer is  reference tiles. A reference tile simply means creating another copy of the same tile in a different group, without impacting the original app. Think of it like sharing the same tile with multiple user groups.

For example, let’s say the tile is already available for Group 1, and they’re actively using the application. Now you want Group 2 to access the same tile as well. Instead of creating a new tile or modifying the original, you can just create a reference tile for Group 2. This way, both groups see the same app tile, and any changes to the main app remain unaffected.

In this blog, I’m using the standard app “Maintain Data Sources” to demonstrate how this works.

Benefits

  • One original app
  • Multiple "shortcuts" pointing to it
  •  Update once, works everywhere
  • No duplicate maintenance 

Note: it's not copy of tile it is a pointer of original application 

Step 1: Find Your Application
Click the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library  and search for your application.

Step 2: Copy the Business Catalog
In the configuration, copy the business catalog. In that catalog, only the tile will be there.

Step 3: Locate the Tile in Copied Catalog
Once you've copied and pasted the catalog, search within it. The tile will be there, and now you can create a reference.

Step 4: Drag and Drop for Reference
After finding the tile, you need to drag it. Two options will appear: Reference and Delete. Drop the tile on Reference.

Step 5: Select Target Catalog
Once you drop it at reference, it will ask which catalog you want to create the reference in. Select your target catalog.

Step 6: Confirmation Message
After selecting the catalog, you'll receive a "Reference Created" message as shown in the screenshot.

Step 7: Create Target Mapping Reference 
After completing the tile reference, you must create a reference for target mapping - otherwise, it won't navigate anywhere!
Go to Target Mapping and select the mapping. In the bottom toolbar, click Create Reference.

 

Step 8: Select Same Catalog for Target Mapping
After clicking Create Reference, select the same catalog as the tile. You'll receive confirmation that the reference was created in the catalog.

Step 9: Now you can verify that the tile is created in the targeted catalog and works properly.

Capture in Transport Request (TR)** - Don't forget to capture this in a TR, otherwise you can't transport it to other systems!

Conclusion:  Reference tiles are like having a clone button that doesn't create maintenance. You can share the same application across multiple user groups without the complexity of managing duplicate versions.

.

entry.title: SAP Build Work Zone: Tile Opens(content federation) but App Fails with ‘IP Address Cannot Be Found
entry.content:
In my SAP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition) environment, I faced an issue where a dynamic tile loaded successfully through content federation, but clicking the tile caused the application to fail with a browser error:

ERR_NAME_NOT_RESOLVED – The IP address of the server could not be found

The failing URL always contained a very long auto‑generated hostname.

 

Interestingly, the dynamic tile itself worked correctly (e.g., OData count displayed), but navigating to the corresponding SAP Fiori app consistently failed. This clearly indicated that the issue was not with backend connectivity or destinations, but with the content runtime URL generated by Work Zone.
 
Please refer the screenshots.

entry.title: UI5con Bengaluru 2026 - Community, Code, and the Future of SAP UI Development
entry.content:
UI5con Bengaluru 2026 brought together 400+ developers, architects, consultants, and students from across India for a full day of learning, hands-on exploration, and community connection. Held at the SAP Labs campus in Devanahalli as part of SAP Inside Track Bengaluru 2026, the event marked a historic double first — the first time UI5con has taken place in India in seven years, and the very first SAP Inside Track and UI5con held jointly at this location. The event covered everything from the cutting-edge world of AI-assisted development to the fundamentals of accessibility and enterprise UX — all through the lens of SAPUI5 and SAP Fiori.

This was more than a conference; it was a homecoming. With UI5con returning to India after seven years and joining forces with SAP Inside Track for the very first time, the energy in the room was something special — a community reunited and ready to build.

"It's my first SAP Inside Track session as an ABAP Fiori developer. I had a great session attending UI5con. Learned some amazing things during the session."
Participant, Deloitte USI

Kickoff: Agentic Coding with UI5 — From Idea to Inclusive App

Stefan Beck, Peter Muessig, and Rajashree Raghavendra opened the day with a keynote that was equal parts history lesson and vision statement — reminding the audience that UI5con Bengaluru began in 2018, grew to 400+ attendees, 28 sessions, and 41 speakers in 2019, and is now back after a COVID-forced pause of seven years. They traced SAPUI5’s journey from its 2012 enterprise foundations to its current form: TypeScript, Web Components, CI/CD, and a 90% accessibility score in the Web Almanac 2025. Two strategic bets anchored the roadmap: UI5 Web Components (framework-free, standards-based) and Legacy-Free OpenUI5/SAPUI5 (deprecated code removed, async by default).

The AI portion of the keynote charted the evolution from basic code completion in 2020 to the agentic coding era of today and introduced the UI5 MCP Server and Fiori MCP Server as the practical bridges between AI tools and SAP development best practices. A live demo closed the session — an inclusive app built from scratch using Fiori elements Building Blocks and Freestyle code, guided by agentic AI in real time. The vision set the tone for everything that followed: "UI5 in 2026 is where AI acceleration, inclusive design, and enterprise trust meet."

AI Takes Centre Stage

If there was one theme that ran through every session at UI5con 2026, it was AI — and not just as a buzzword. The hands-on session on the MCP Server for Fiori, led by Vinay Hospete, Ashish Anand, and Maheeth Reddy, introduced attendees to the Model Context Protocol — the emerging standard that connects AI coding agents to domain-specific tools and best practices. The demo pod on Vibe Coding with SAP UI5 brought this to life, showing how natural language prompts combined with Cline and SAP AI Core enable governed, auditable UI5 app generation — where AI accelerates the work, but the developer stays in control.

"Vibe coding: Using the MCP server, the code can be generated quickly and can be adapted for the demo & quick mockup."
Participant, Nokia

Deep Dives: Going Beyond the Basics

The deep dive track gave attendees space to go further. Merlin Beutlberger's UI5 in the World of AI showcased the UI5 MCP Server's 10 tools — from app creation and API reference lookups to Linter runs and TypeScript migration guides — with a live demo of an AI agent building a full UI5 app from a natural language prompt. Jitendra Kansal's MDK session addressed how to bring Fiori to mobile without a rewrite, with the MDK MCP Server intelligently transforming UI for smaller screens. Mohammed Sijas introduced the DataRepository library, a lightweight solution that brings OData-like CRUD convenience to plain REST APIs.

Sandeep R S and Vinay Hospete reinforced that ~90% of SAP's own SAPUI5 apps are built on Fiori elements, and that MCP-powered agentic AI is the natural next step. Syed's session on Fiori Applications Demystified gave backend developers and architects a grounding in MVC, bindings, and when to choose Freestyle over Fiori Elements.

"Good place to know what's new and what is the future scope. Hands-on sessions give good insight on the topics."
Participant, Cargill

Accessibility, Web Components, and More

Gergana Georgieva's session on UI5's Ever-Evolving Accessibility was a highlight for many attendees. She walked through SAP's commitment to WCAG 2.2 and ARIA 1.2 standards, covering everything from keyboard navigation and screen reader support to the new Target Size requirements. The key message: accessibility requires both framework support and developer discipline — it must become a habit, not an afterthought.

"I really liked the accessibility session."
Participant, Wipro

The demo pod on UI5 Web Components — presented by Rounak, Vignesh, and Dharshini — showcased the growing maturity of the framework-agnostic component library built on web standards. Web Components 2.0 was announced, with new components including the intriguing <ui5-ai-button>. For developers working with React, Angular, or Vue, this opens new doors.

"It was very interactive. Now they are releasing web components 2.0 which looks promising."
Participant, Adidas

The Suite Controls session from Ashwin Kumar Bolar, Vinay K N, and Santhosh covered enterprise-grade components for complex business applications: Network Graph, UploadSetWithTable, and the Gantt Chart — each purpose-built for the kind of rich operational dashboards that SAP customers depend on. Kotresh T M rounded out the day with a deep dive into cross-app navigation for Fiori microfrontends, making the case for intent-based navigation as the standard pattern for loosely coupled Fiori apps.

 And for those excited about the future of AI in SAP applications, Arun Bharathi K's demo on Joule Frontend Actions showed how UI5 apps can expose capabilities directly to SAP's digital AI assistant — making Joule a conversational interface for everyday Fiori interactions.

"Got to know about the capability of Joule with the frontend actions. Would like to have a blog on this."
Participant, AMGBT

Hands-On Sessions: Learning by Doing

A major draw of UI5con has always been its hands-on sessions — and 2026 was no exception. Attendees rolled up their sleeves and built real UI5 apps, working through exercises on SAP Fiori elements V4, the Flexible Programming Model, and the UI5 Linter, with GitHub repositories shared so participants could take the work home. Volunteers circulated the room throughout, ensuring no one got stuck, and the atmosphere — equal parts focused and buzzing — was one of the highlights of the day.

“Attended 2 hands-on sessions of UI5con. Volunteers are always ready to help, amazing knowledgeable speakers. Loved the UI5con event.”
Participant, Deloitte

Ask the Expert: The Closing Highlight

The day closed with one of its most anticipated moments: the Ask the Expert panel. Peter Muessig, Vinod S Nair, Stefan Beck and Sandeep R S — some of the most prominent names behind UI5 and SAP Fiori — took to the stage together for an open, free-flowing conversation with the audience. The session was part Q&A, part fireside chat, and completely candid. Attendees asked about the roadmap, the future of legacy migrations, how best to adopt agentic AI in their own teams, and much more. It was exactly the kind of direct access to the people building the platform that makes UI5con special — and a fitting send-off for the day.

The Community Makes the Event

Across the feedback forms, one theme stood out as clearly as the technical content: people valued the community. Volunteers were praised for their patience, speakers for making complex topics accessible, and the hands-on format — with GitHub links, live demos, and side-by-side coding — gave participants something concrete to take home.

Looking Ahead

UI5con Bengaluru 2026 made one thing clear: seven years was too long to wait. The SAP UI5 ecosystem is moving faster than ever, the community is hungry for it, and Devanahalli proved it can host a world-class developer event. Thank you to every speaker, volunteer, and attendee who made this happen. See you at the next one.

"Looking forward for the next iteration of UI5con."
Participant, Volvo

entry.title: IoT: Air Quality Dashboard with SAP RAP and Mobile App
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!

After my last blog about IoT and SAP CAP, I’d like to share a new pet project combining IoT and SAP ABAP RAP.
Last summer was extremely hot, and I kept wondering when it would be best to open the windows in our meeting room to get some fresh air. That led me to another question: could I measure the air quality and integrate the data with SAP Cloud Platform?
Elevated concentrations of carbon dioxide (CO2) and insufficient ventilation in indoor environments can decrease cognitive performance and may cause headaches. CO2 detectors are used to monitor concentrations and provide a warning when predefined thresholds are exceeded. Carbon dioxide concentration is expressed in ppm (parts per million) which represents the number of carbon dioxide molecules per one million molecules of air.

Indoor air quality is classified according to the following concentration ranges:

  • at concentration below 800ppm, air quality is considered good;
  • between 800 and 1000ppm, slight stuffiness may be perceived;
  • between 1000 and 1500ppm, air quality is poor and cognitive performance may decrease;
  • between 1500 and 2000ppm, headaches and drowsiness may occur, and ventilation is recommended;
  • above 2000ppm indicate significantly elevated CO2 levels and require immediate ventilation to improve indoor air quality;

So, I decided to build the IoT device on my favorite Raspberry Pi platform. For this project, I chose the fourth-generation Raspberry Pi 4 single-board computer.
Also I found a CO₂ sensor that produces an analog voltage signal. To process this data digitally, it first needs to be converted using an analog-to-digital converter (ADC).

1.Hardware part

Below are the links to the devices I used:

CO2 Sensor SKU SEN0159 

DFRobot I2C ADS1115 

All components are connected.

 

The GPIO shows which wire connects to which pin:

Actually, it looks as follows:

After many tries, I combined the example programs from both devices into a Python script to test the sensor.

A quick (and possibly boring) theory about the calibration curve:

The sensor outputs a voltage that decreases as CO₂ concentration increases. To convert voltage to ppm, we use a logarithmic calibration curve:

Where:

  • log_400 = 2.602 → log10(400 ppm), the baseline for clean air.
  • V_zero = ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE
  • slope = REACTION_VOLTAGE / (log_400 - log_1000)

This allows us to map measured voltages to actual CO₂ concentrations in ppm.

import time
import math
import sys
import os

sys.path.append(os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(__file__))))
from DFRobot_ADS1115 import ADS1115

# Device parameters
MG_PIN = 0  # define which analog input channel you are going to user
DC_GAIN = 8.5  # define the DC gain of amplifier

# Programm parameters
READ_SAMPLE_INTERVAL = 0.05  # Interval between measurements (sec.)
READ_SAMPLE_TIMES = 5  # Sample numbers

# Calibration
ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE = 0.2936  # Default street voltage for 400ppm
REACTION_VOLTAGE = 0.030  # Voltage drop 1000ppm
CO2Curve = [2.602, ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE, (REACTION_VOLTAGE / (2.602 - 3))]

# Initialization ADS1115
ads1115 = ADS1115()
ads1115.set_addr_ADS1115(0x48)  # Set I2C adress
ads1115.set_gain(0x00)  # Set gain 6.144V

def read_adc(channel):
    """Reading ADS1115"""
    adc_val = ads1115.read_voltage(channel)
    return adc_val['r'] / 1000.0  # Transform to volts

def mg_read(mg_pin):
    """Reading average voltage"""
    v = 0
    for _ in range(READ_SAMPLE_TIMES):
        v += read_adc(mg_pin)
        time.sleep(READ_SAMPLE_INTERVAL)
    v = v / READ_SAMPLE_TIMES
    return v

def mg_get_percentage(volts, pcurve):
    """Measurement CO₂ concentration в ppm"""
    if (volts / DC_GAIN) >= ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE:
        return -1
    else:
        return 10 ** (((volts / DC_GAIN) - pcurve[1]) / pcurve[2] + pcurve[0])

def ppm_to_percent(ppm):
    """Calculate ppm to percents"""
    return ppm / 10000.0  # 1 ppm = 0.0001%

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("MG-811 Demo")
    try:
        while True:
            volts = mg_read(MG_PIN)
            print(f"SEN0159: {volts:.3f}V", end="  ")
            
            percentage = mg_get_percentage(volts, CO2Curve)
            if percentage == -1:
                print("CO2: <400 ppm (<0.04%)")
            else:
                percent = ppm_to_percent(percentage)
                print(f"CO2: {int(percentage)} ppm ({percent:.4f}%)")
            
            time.sleep(0.5)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        print("Program terminated by user")

Key Parameters from the script:

  • MG_PIN = 0 – Analog input channel for the MG-811 sensor.
  • DC_GAIN = 8.5 – Amplifier gain of the sensor.
  • READ_SAMPLE_INTERVAL = 0.05 s – Time between measurements.
  • READ_SAMPLE_TIMES = 5 – Number of samples to average.
  • ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE = 0.2936 V – Voltage at 400 ppm CO₂ (clean air).
  • REACTION_VOLTAGE = 0.030 V – Voltage drop at 1000 ppm CO₂.

To determine ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE, I put the sensor outside in clean air.
